cot_prefix: Okay, let's tackle this question. The question is asking what made Woolworth a world-famous man. The options are A, B, C, or D. Let me go through the passage again to make sure I understand the key points. First, the passage starts by mentioning Frank W. Woolworth's background. He was born in a poor farming family and didn't want to be a farmer. He took a business course and became a salesman. His natural skill was displaying goods to attract interest. The pivotal moment was when he sold odds and ends at five cents each, which was very successful. Then, he opened his own store with five and ten-cent items. But he learned another lesson: to buy in large quantities directly from factories. The example given is his order of knives in Germany, which was so large it cut the price in half. By 1919, he had over 1,000 stores and expanded to London. He became famous and made millions, with his rule that "the customer is always right." The question is about what made him world-famous. Let's look at the options: A. His business skills and his wealth B. The low price of the goods he sold C. His trip to Germany and his huge order of knives D. His natural skill for displaying things The passage mentions his business acumen, like buying in bulk to lower prices, his expansion, and his famous rule. His wealth and business success led to his fame. Option A combines his skills and wealth. Option B is part of his strategy but not the reason for his fame. The knife order (C) was an example of his strategy but not the main reason. Option D was a skill he had early on but the fame came
